Here is an initial fact sheet to consider:

1. GIVEN OR VERIFIED FACTS  
   - The poem in question is “Father Son and Holy Ghost” by Audre Lorde.

2. FACTS TO LOOK UP  
   - The specific stanza number in the poem where some lines are indented. This may be found in a published collection of Audre Lorde's poetry or on an authoritative literary website.

3. FACTS TO DERIVE

4. EDUCATED GUESSES  
   - It is possible that the poem may have 3 to 5 stanzas, as many contemporary poems fall within this range, but this is purely a guess and should be verified by looking up the text.

About 312,000 resultsThe full text of Audre Lorde's poem "Father Son and Holy Ghost" can be found on various poetry websites, including Best Poems.
Here is the text of the poem:Father Son and Holy Ghost I have not ever seen my father’s grave. as we pass. calling it weed. but one. a January 15th that year me. I have not ever seen my father’s grave. Not that his judgment eyes have been forgotten nor his great hands’ print on our evening doorknobs one half turn each night and he would come drabbled with the world’s business massive and silent as the whole day’s wish ready to redefine each of our shapes but now the evening doorknobs wait and do not recognize us as we pass. Each week a different woman regular as his one quick glass each evening pulls up the grass his stillness grows calling it weed. Each week a different woman has my mother’s face and he who time has changeless must be amazed who knew and loved but one. My father died in silence. For the full text and more information about the poem, you can visit Best Poems or Poetry Foundation. Poetry Foundation

Father Son and Holy Ghost
By 
Audre LordeI have not ever seen my father’s grave.
 Not that his judgment eyes
 have been forgotten
 nor his great hands’ print
 on our evening doorknobs
 one half turn each night
 and he would come
 drabbled with the world’s business 
 massive and silent
 as the whole day’s wish 
 ready to redefine
 each of our shapes
 but now the evening doorknobs 
 wait and do not recognize us 
 as we pass.
 Each week a different woman 
 regular as his one quick glass
Share
More About This Poem

Please identify which stanza in 'Father Son and Holy Ghost' contains indented lines and provide the stanza number.

In Audre Lorde’s poem "Father Son and Holy Ghost," the stanza with indented lines is the third stanza. This stanza reflects an ongoing cycle of women who come into the speaker's life, depicted through the imagery of different women pulling up grass that signifies their father's stillness, indicating a deep sense of loss and the impact of absence. The indentation visually emphasizes the lines, which contribute to the poem's rhythm and thematic exploration of memory, loss, and identity.

The poem overall grapples with the complexities of familial relationships, the enduring presence of the speaker's father in her life despite his physical absence, and the reflections on how different figures take on roles influenced by the speaker's mother. The indentation in this specific stanza highlights the contrast between the generational relationships and the speaker's emotional struggles, serving to enrich the poem's expressive depth.

In Audre Lorde's poem "Father Son and Holy Ghost," the stanza with indented lines is the third stanza. This indentation contributes to the poem's rhythm and emphasizes the themes of memory, loss, and identity present throughout the work.
